items.html:
Welcome to Personal Care

Welcome to this awareness course on personal care. Personal care means supporting someone with the everyday, intimate tasks of looking after their own body — washing, dressing, using the toilet, and caring for skin, hair, nails and mouth. For most people these are private moments, and how you help with them says a great deal about the respect you show. This course is for care workers in England and is grounded in the values of the Care Certificate and good-practice guidance.

  • Dignity first: personal care touches the most private parts of a person's life.
  • Your role: to support, never to take over, and always to follow the care plan.
  • Awareness: this is knowledge, and your practice must also be seen at work.

This e-learning covers the knowledge element only. Being able to deliver personal care safely and kindly must also be observed by your employer, who watches your competence in real work before signing it off. An online course alone cannot confirm that you are ready.

What this course covers

Over the next pages you will move from the values behind personal care to the practical skill of delivering it well.

  • Values: dignity, privacy, respect, consent, choice, independence and cultural needs.
  • Preparing and delivering: consent, communication, the right environment, infection control and independence.
  • The main areas: washing, mouth care, grooming, dressing, toileting and skin care.

The final section looks at how you observe, report and record what you notice. Throughout, remember that personal care is delivered according to each person's care plan, and that any change you see is reported so the plan can be kept up to date. Keep your own workplace and the people you support in mind as you learn.

Dignity, privacy and respect

Dignity is at the heart of personal care. To act with dignity is to treat someone as a valued human being, whatever help they need. When you support a person to wash or dress, you are helping with something they would normally do alone and unseen, so small acts of thoughtfulness matter enormously.

  • Dignity: valuing the person and protecting their self-respect.
  • Privacy: keeping their body, their space and their information private.
  • Respect: treating their wishes, background and choices as important.

These values are not extra steps to fit in when there is time. They shape every part of good personal care, and they cost nothing but attention.

Protecting privacy in practice

Privacy is easy to overlook when you are busy, yet it is one of the things people say matters most to them. Simple actions protect it: closing doors and curtains, covering the parts of the body you are not washing, and knocking and waiting before you enter a room.

Privacy also means keeping what you learn about a person confidential. During personal care you may see or hear things they would not share widely — a scar, a worry, a moment of embarrassment. Treat all of it with discretion, sharing only what colleagues need to know to give safe care. Protecting a person's privacy tells them, without words, that you see them as a whole person and not just a task to complete.

items.question: A person is embarrassed about needing help to wash. Which approach best protects their dignity and privacy?

answers.text: Close the door and curtains, cover the parts of the body you are not washing, and explain each step as you go
answers.feedback: Correct. Privacy, keeping the person covered, and gentle explanation all protect their dignity.

answers.text: Work as quickly as possible and leave the door open so you can call for help
answers.feedback: Not quite. Leaving the door open exposes the person and does not respect their privacy.

answers.text: Chat to a colleague about the person's difficulties while you work
answers.feedback: No. Discussing the person's difficulties in front of them breaches confidentiality and dignity.

answers.text: Tell the person there is nothing to be embarrassed about and carry on without explaining
answers.feedback: Dismissing someone's feelings and not explaining what you are doing does not protect their dignity.

items.html:
Consent, choice and independence

No one has the right to touch another person without their agreement, and personal care is no exception. Consent means the person understands what you are offering and freely agrees to it. Alongside consent sit choice and independence — the person's right to have a say and to do as much as they can for themselves.

  • Consent: gained before you begin and checked as you go.
  • Choice: a say in how, when and by whom care is given.
  • Independence: doing for themselves what they are able to do.

Honouring these values changes personal care from something done to a person into something done with them.

Cultural and religious needs

People's backgrounds shape how they wish to be cared for, and personal care is an area where this matters deeply. A person may prefer a carer of the same sex, may follow particular routines for washing before prayer, or may have customs around hair, modesty, or the use of the right and left hand. Some have grooming and dietary practices tied to their faith.

You are not expected to know every custom, but you are expected to ask, listen and respect. The care plan should record a person's cultural and religious wishes, and you should follow them just as carefully as any other part of their care. Where you are unsure, ask the person or their family kindly rather than assuming. Respecting who someone is, including their faith and culture, is part of respecting their dignity.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the sentences about values in personal care.
items.textField: Always gain a person's *consent* before you begin, and offer them *choice* about how and when care is given. Support their *independence* by letting them do what they can for themselves, and respect their *cultural* and religious wishes as recorded in the care plan.

items.html:
Gaining and checking consent

Consent is not a single moment at the start of a task; it runs through the whole of personal care. Before you begin, explain what you would like to do and ask if that is alright. As you work, keep checking — a person can change their mind at any point, and you must respect that.

  • Informed: the person understands what you are offering and why.
  • Freely given: they agree without pressure or being rushed.
  • Ongoing: you keep checking they are still happy to continue.

Consent can be spoken, or shown through a nod, a gesture or body language. Learning to read these signals is an important part of the job.

Communicating well

Good personal care depends on good communication. Speak clearly and warmly, at the person's pace, and tell them what you are going to do before you do it — “I am going to help you wash your back now, is that alright?” This keeps them informed and in control, and prevents the startle of being touched unexpectedly.

Communication is two-way. Watch the person's face and body for signs of pain, cold, tiredness or discomfort, especially if they cannot easily tell you in words. Give them time to respond, and never talk over them or about them as though they were not there. If someone communicates differently — through pictures, signs, or a communication aid — use the methods recorded in their care plan so their voice is always heard.

items.question: Part way through helping someone shower, they say they would like to stop. What should you do?

answers.text: Stop, respect their decision, check they are safe and comfortable, and report the refusal so it can be recorded
answers.feedback: Correct. Consent is ongoing; you stop, keep the person safe, and report and record the refusal.

answers.text: Carry on quickly because the task is nearly finished
answers.feedback: No. Continuing after someone withdraws consent is never acceptable, however nearly finished you are.

answers.text: Tell them they must continue because it is written in their care plan
answers.feedback: Not quite. A care plan does not override a person's right to withdraw consent at any time.

answers.text: Leave them alone in the shower and come back later without telling anyone
answers.feedback: No. Leaving them unsafe and telling no one puts the person at risk and fails to report the refusal.

items.html:
Preparing the environment

Where and how personal care takes place affects how safe and dignified it feels. A little preparation makes everything go more smoothly and protects the person from cold, embarrassment and risk. Before you start, get the space and everything you need ready.

  • Privacy: close doors, curtains and blinds before anyone undresses.
  • Warmth: make sure the room and any water are at a comfortable temperature.
  • Everything to hand: gather towels, soap, clean clothes and equipment first.

Preparing well means you will not have to leave a person exposed or alone while you fetch something you forgot.

Safety and comfort

Personal care often takes place in bathrooms and around water, which can be slippery and hazardous. Check that the floor is dry, that any grab rails and equipment are sound, and that the water is a safe, comfortable temperature before the person gets in — test it yourself or with a thermometer, as very hot water can scald.

Think about comfort as well as safety. Warm the towels if you can, keep the person covered as much as possible, and uncover only the part of the body you are caring for. If you use any equipment such as a hoist or shower chair, follow your moving-and-handling training and the person's care plan. A calm, warm, well-prepared environment tells the person they are cared for and safe.

items.question: You should test the temperature of the water before a person gets into a bath or shower.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Always check the water is comfortable and safe first, as water that is too hot can scald.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. You must always check the water temperature first — water that is too hot can cause serious scalds.

items.html:
Preventing the spread of infection

Personal care brings you into close contact with people and with bodily fluids, so infection prevention and control protects both you and the people you support. Good habits stop germs passing from one person to another, which matters greatly when the people you care for may be frail or unwell.

  • Hand hygiene: the single most important way to prevent infection.
  • PPE: gloves and aprons used correctly for the task.
  • Clean as you go: safe handling of laundry, waste and equipment.

These are everyday routines, but doing them properly every time is what keeps people safe.

Hand hygiene and PPE

Handwashing is the foundation of infection control. Wash your hands with soap and water before and after giving personal care, after using the toilet, and whenever they are visibly dirty, following your workplace's technique. Where sinks are not to hand, use the hand sanitiser your employer provides.

Personal protective equipment — usually disposable gloves and aprons for personal care — adds a barrier when you may touch bodily fluids. Put on fresh PPE for each task and each person, remove it carefully to avoid contaminating yourself, and dispose of it in the correct waste. Never reuse single-use gloves. Deal with soiled laundry and clinical waste according to your employer's policy. Following these steps protects the person, your colleagues, your own family and you.

items.question: When should you carry out hand hygiene during personal care?

answers.text: Before and after giving personal care, after using the toilet, and whenever your hands are visibly dirty
answers.feedback: Correct. Hand hygiene at these key moments is the most effective way to prevent the spread of infection.

answers.text: Only at the start of your shift
answers.feedback: No. Washing once at the start of a shift does not protect people through the tasks that follow.

answers.text: Only if the person appears to be unwell
answers.feedback: Not quite. Germs spread even when no one looks unwell, so hand hygiene is always needed.

answers.text: Only after you have finished all your visits for the day
answers.feedback: No. Hand hygiene must happen around each task, not just once at the end of the day.

items.html:
Promoting independence

Good personal care supports a person to do as much as they can for themselves, rather than doing everything for them. This is not about saving time — it is about protecting a person's confidence, skills and sense of who they are. Being able to wash your own face or fasten your own buttons matters.

  • Offer, don't take over: let the person try before you step in.
  • Adapt: use aids and gentle prompts so tasks stay within reach.
  • Encourage: give time and praise rather than rushing.

The care plan will show what a person can do and where they need help, so support is pitched just right.

Offering real choice

Choice is a form of respect. Wherever you can, offer the person a genuine say in their personal care: whether they would like a bath or a shower, which clothes to wear, which soap or scent they prefer, and when in the day they would like their care. Small choices restore a sense of control to someone who may feel they have lost a good deal of it.

Offer choices in a way the person can respond to — hold up two tops rather than asking an open question, if that helps. Respect the answer, even when it is not the choice you would make, as long as it is safe. Where a routine is set out in the care plan, there is usually still room for choice within it. Being offered choices, and having them honoured, helps a person feel they are still the author of their own life.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the guidance on independence and choice.
items.textField: Promote a person's *independence* by letting them do what they can for themselves before you step in. Offer real *choice* about how and when their care is given, and *respect* their decision even when it differs from yours. Follow the *care* plan, which shows where support is needed.

items.html:
Washing, bathing and showering

Helping someone stay clean and fresh supports their health, comfort and self-esteem. How much help a person needs varies widely — some manage with a little encouragement, others need full support — and the care plan sets this out. Whatever the level of help, the same values of dignity, privacy and choice apply.

  • Ask first: agree what help the person wants and how.
  • Keep covered: wash and dry one area at a time, covering the rest.
  • Be gentle: use warm water and mild products, and dry thoroughly.

A wash can be a bath, a shower or a supported wash in bed — whatever suits the person and their needs that day.

A safe and dignified wash

Prepare the environment and gather everything first, so the person is never left cold or exposed. Check the water temperature, help the person into a safe position, and encourage them to wash the parts they can reach themselves. Work gently and unhurriedly, watching their face for any sign of discomfort.

Take particular care to wash and dry skin folds, between the toes, and the genital and bottom area, as moisture left here can cause soreness and infection. Always wash from the cleanest to the least clean area. Pat the skin dry rather than rubbing, and notice its condition as you go — this is a good chance to spot any redness or broken skin. When you have finished, help the person into clean, comfortable clothes and leave them warm and settled.

items.question: While helping someone wash, why is it important to dry carefully between skin folds and toes?

answers.text: Moisture left on the skin can lead to soreness, breakdown and infection
answers.feedback: Correct. Damp skin in folds and between toes can become sore and broken, so thorough drying matters.

answers.text: It makes the wash quicker to finish
answers.feedback: No. Careful drying is about protecting the skin, not about speed.

answers.text: It is only important for people who can walk
answers.feedback: Not quite. Skin needs protecting for everyone, whether or not they are able to walk.

answers.text: Drying is not necessary if the room is warm
answers.feedback: No. A warm room does not remove moisture from skin folds; you must still dry carefully.

items.html:
Mouth care, hair and nails

Personal care is about far more than washing. Caring for the mouth, hair and nails keeps a person comfortable, healthy and feeling like themselves. Oral care in particular is easily overlooked, yet a sore or dry mouth can cause real pain and make eating difficult.

  • Mouth care: help with brushing teeth or cleaning dentures twice a day.
  • Hair: support washing, brushing and styling to the person's liking.
  • Nails: keep them clean, and follow policy on who may cut nails.

Always follow the care plan — for example, some people's nails, such as those of a person with diabetes, should only be cut by a specialist.

Support with dressing

Getting dressed is deeply personal. What we wear expresses who we are, so involve the person at every step. Offer a genuine choice of clothing, respecting their taste, the weather and any cultural or religious dress they prefer. Lay clothes out in order and encourage the person to do as much as they can themselves.

Work at the person's pace and keep them covered as you go, dressing the weaker or more painful side first when someone has limited movement on one side. Check that clothing is comfortable, not twisted or rubbing, and that fastenings are done up. Well-chosen, comfortable clothes that the person has helped select support both their dignity and their independence. As always, notice and report anything that concerns you, such as clothes that no longer fit or skin that looks sore.

items.question: When helping someone with weakness on one side to dress, you should put clothing onto the weaker side first.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Dressing the weaker or more painful side first is gentler and easier for the person.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. You should dress the weaker or more affected side first, as this is more comfortable and easier.

items.html:
Toileting and continence support

Helping someone use the toilet, or supporting them with continence, calls for great sensitivity. These are among the most private of all personal care tasks, and a person may feel embarrassed or upset. Your calm, matter-of-fact and respectful manner makes an enormous difference.

  • Respond promptly: answer requests for the toilet without delay.
  • Privacy: give as much privacy as is safe at every stage.
  • Hygiene: clean and dry the skin well, and wear appropriate PPE.

Follow the person's continence care plan, and never make someone feel they are a nuisance for needing help.

Skin care and pressure damage

Skin is easily harmed when a person sits or lies in one position for a long time, or when skin stays damp. This can lead to pressure ulcers (also called pressure sores), which are painful and serious but often preventable. Personal care is a key chance to protect the skin and to spot early warning signs.

Keep skin clean and dry, change soiled pads or clothing promptly, and help the person change position regularly if their care plan says so. As you wash and dress someone, look closely at the skin, especially over bony areas such as the heels, hips, base of the spine and elbows. Early signs include redness that does not fade when pressed, or areas that are unusually warm, hard or painful. If you see any of these, do not massage the area — report it straight away so action can be taken.

items.question: While helping someone wash, you notice redness over the base of their spine that does not fade when you press it. What should you do?

answers.text: Avoid massaging the area and report it promptly so it can be assessed and acted on
answers.feedback: Correct. Non-fading redness can be an early pressure ulcer; do not massage it, and report it straight away.

answers.text: Rub the area firmly to improve the blood flow
answers.feedback: No. Massaging or rubbing damaged skin can make pressure damage worse and should be avoided.

answers.text: Ignore it, as redness is always harmless
answers.feedback: Not quite. Redness that does not fade when pressed can be an early sign of serious pressure damage.

answers.text: Wait a few days to see whether it gets worse before telling anyone
answers.feedback: No. Pressure damage can develop quickly, so it must be reported promptly, not left to worsen.

items.html:
Noticing changes

Because you help a person with such close, regular care, you are often the first to notice when something changes. This makes you an important pair of eyes for the whole care team. A change may be physical, or it may be in how a person seems in themselves.

  • Skin: redness, broken skin, rashes, bruising or swelling.
  • Body: weight loss or gain, poor appetite, or new pain.
  • Mood and ability: low mood, confusion, or new difficulty with tasks.

None of these needs a diagnosis from you — your job is simply to notice and to pass it on.

Why small changes matter

A change that seems minor can be the first sign of something that needs attention — an infection, dehydration, a pressure ulcer beginning to form, or a person becoming unwell or unhappy. Someone who is normally chatty falling quiet, or who suddenly needs more help than usual, is telling you something even without words.

Because you see the person regularly, you come to know what is normal for them, and that makes you well placed to spot what is not. Trust your instinct: if something feels different or not right, it is always worth reporting. It is far better to mention a change that turns out to be nothing than to stay silent about one that mattered. Reporting early gives the team the best chance to help the person quickly.

items.question: You notice that a person who is usually cheerful and chatty has become quiet and withdrawn over a few visits. What is the right thing to do?

answers.text: Report the change so the team can look into it, even though you cannot say what is causing it
answers.feedback: Correct. A change in mood can be an early sign of something wrong, so report it for the team to explore.

answers.text: Say nothing, as mood is not part of personal care
answers.feedback: No. Changes in mood and manner are important and should always be noticed and reported.

answers.text: Wait until the next monthly review to mention it
answers.feedback: Not quite. Waiting weeks could delay help; changes should be reported promptly.

answers.text: Decide it must be nothing and forget about it
answers.feedback: No. It is not for you to dismiss a change; report it so the team can decide whether it matters.

items.html:
Respecting refusals

A person has every right to refuse personal care, or part of it, even when you feel it would do them good. Refusal must be respected — you cannot force or pressure someone into being washed, dressed or changed. What you can do is offer gently, try again later, and find out what lies behind the “no”.

  • Stay calm: do not argue, rush or shame the person.
  • Explore: they may be in pain, tired, cold, frightened or embarrassed.
  • Report and record: pass on repeated or worrying refusals.

Respecting a refusal is part of respecting the person — but a pattern of refusal that affects someone's health must always be reported.

Links to safeguarding and safe moving

Personal care connects closely to keeping people safe from harm. As you help someone wash or dress, you may notice unexplained bruises, injuries, poor conditions, or signs that a person is frightened or neglected. These can be indicators of abuse or neglect, and you have a duty to report any safeguarding concern straight away, following your employer's policy — never keep it to yourself.

Personal care also often involves helping people move — into a bath, on and off the toilet, or turning in bed to protect their skin. Always follow your moving-and-handling training and the person's care plan, using the equipment provided. Never improvise a lift or put yourself or the person at risk to save time. Working safely protects the person from injury and protects you too. When you are unsure, stop and ask.

Keeping accurate records

Reporting what you notice by word of mouth is important, but it must also be written down. Accurate records mean the whole team knows what has happened, care stays consistent, and any change can be tracked over time. Good records are also a legal requirement, and they protect both the person and you.

  • Accurate: write what you actually saw or did, not what you assume.
  • Timely: record as soon as you can, while it is fresh.
  • Clear and factual: avoid opinion, and keep records confidential and secure.

Record the personal care you gave, any refusals, and any changes you noticed, following your workplace's system.

Bringing it together

Well done for completing this awareness course on personal care. You have explored what personal care is and the values of dignity, privacy, consent and choice that run through it; how to prepare and deliver care safely, including consent, communication, the environment, infection control and independence; the main areas of care from washing to skin protection; and how to observe, report and record what you notice. Throughout, personal care is delivered according to the person's care plan, and any change is reported.

Remember the key point from the start: this e-learning covers the knowledge element only. Being able to deliver personal care safely, kindly and competently must also be observed by your employer in your real work before it is signed off. An online course alone cannot confirm your competence. Speak to your manager about the workplace observation and assessment that go alongside this learning — and carry these values into every person you support.
